coredata.dc:title: Recent advances on cadmium free quantum dots-liquid crystal nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Supreet,
Singh, Gautam
coredata.dc:description: Liquid crystal (LC) nanoscience has been witnessing a paradigm shift towards the dispersion of Cadmium (Cd) based quantum dots (QDs) into LCs to enhance their electro-optical properties and produce highly efficient tunable electro-optical devices. However, the severe toxicity of Cd based QDs impedes their technological impact on the LC based tunable electro-optical devices and hence demands for the usage of Cd-free QDs. Here, we present a critical review on recent accomplishments and insights in the Cd-free QDs doped LC nanocomposites. The investigations on these nanocomposites have clearly demonstrated that the core/shell structure QDs (CIS/ZnS, InP/ZnS and Co-ZnO/ZnO), ZnO and ZnS based QDs, Carbon Dots, and perovskite-quantum dots possess the strong potentials to greatly modulate the properties of LCs and hence could be used as alternative eco-friendly dopants. Moreover, the focus has been made on summarizing how the size and shape dependent optical and electronic properties of Cd-free QDs influence the electrical, electro-optical and photoluminescent properties of different types LCs (i.e. nematic, chiral nematic and ferroelectric). The overview on inclusive existing status, challenges, and future scope of Cd-free QDs-LC nanocomposites has also been discussed. The present review indicates that LC nanocomposites made of Cd-free QDs hold a great promise for their futuristic applications in various robust, low cost, tunable and sustainable electro-optical devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of different phases of matter, namely crystalline solid (complete positional and orientational order), liquid crystal (orientational order and quasi positional order) and isotropic liquid (no order) with variation in temperature is shown.
The density of states for an electron in a semiconductor, represented for 3D (bulk), 2D (slab), 1D (wire) and 0D (dot) systems. [30].
The schematic representation of size dependent photoluminescence of QDs is shown here.

Polarizing optical microscope images (with crossed polarizers) of the LC–QD composite with different filling methods. The doping concentration varies from 0.01 mol L − 1 (a, c, e and g) to 0.02 mol L − 1 (b, d, f and h). Negative dielectric anisotropic LC–QDs show good homeotropic alignment with dropcasting (e and f) and random alignment (a and b) with capillary filling. Reversely, positive dielectric anisotropic LC–QDs exhibit uniform homogeneous alignment (c and d) with capillary filling and poor alignment states (g and h) with dropcasting. The inset in each figure shows the same area with parallel (un-crossed) polarizers. Blue arrows show the capillary filling direction [56].

Polarizing optical micrographs (POMs) of pure FLC (Felix 17/000) and CIS-ZnS QDs-FLC mixtures in SmC* (chiral smectic C at 50Ȃ°C), SmA* (chiral smectic A at 73Ȃ°C) and N* (chiral nematic at 81Ȃ°C) phases. Scale bar is 100 µm in all POMs. The concentration of mixtures i.e. mixture 1 (Mix.1), mixture 2 (Mix.2) and mixture 3 (Mix.3) is 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 wt% [57].

The voltage-dependent (a) cone angle (twice the tilt angle θ) and (b) spontaneous polarization (PS) in neat FLC and CIS-ZnS QDs-FLC mixtures are shown. The insets of (a) and (b) show the concentration-dependent variation in 2θ and PS . The experimental data for the cone angle and spontaneous polarization were theoretically fitted [57].
Temperature dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pure FLC and CIS-ZnS QDs-FLC mixtures [57].
Temperature and concentration dependent birefringence in neat NLC (1823A) and InP/ZnS QDs-NLC composites (composite 1 and composite 2) is shown [64].
Temperature dependent splay elastic constant (K11) for pure NLC (1823A) and InP/ZnS QDs-NLC composites (composite 1 and composite 2) at 4 Hz frequency [64].

Behaviour of optical tilt angle (θ) of pure FLC (KCFLC 7S) and CZZ QDs-FLC composites as a function of applied voltage (V) at room temperature and in the inset, spontaneous polarisation (PS) with respect to concentration of cobalt (Co) in the core of CZZ QDs [65].

Ferroelectric (P-E) hysteresis behavior of pure FLC (KCFLC 7S) and CZZ QDs-FLC composites for 10 Hz at temperature 27 °C. Inset shows spontaneous polarization (PS) as a function of concentration of Co in core of CZZ QDs [66].
Polarising optical micrographs showing the appearance of helical lines in pure FLC (KCFLC 7S) and CZZ QDs-FLC composites at temperature 27 °C [66].
Behavior of (a) PL intensity and (b) shift in the wavelength (Δλ) of PL peak with respect to pure FLC (KCFLC 7S) with the size of ZnO QDs [67].

Three dimensional Cole-Cole plots of mixture 3 at different temperatures. The inset shows the Cole-Cole plot at 72Ȃ°C. Here, ε′ and ε″ denote the real and imaginary parts of the complex dielectric permittivity (ε*). NM and GM stand for new mode and Goldstone mode, respectively [68].
Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (normalized transmittance) of the neat FLC (Felix 17/000) and ZnS QDs doped FLC mixtures at wave number scale at room temperature [68].

(a) De-convoluted spectra for various concentration of ZnS QDs viz. 1, 3, and 5 µl in FLC material KCFLC 7S is shown and the numbers 1, 3, and 5 represent the main and their de-convoluted spectra corresponding to 1, 3, and 5 µl ZnS QDs KCFLC 7S material. (b) Effect of doping concentration of ZnS QDs on emission wavelengths (de-convoluted). The symbols, red solid square and green solid circle represent the variation of low and high wavelength peaks, respectively. Solids are for reference purpose only [69].

Variation in the wavelength of three components of the PL peak with a variation in the concentration of QDs (wt/wt%) in the host FLC (SCE4). Here, 1, 2, 3 refer to PL peak components as depicted in the deconvoluted spectra of an FLC material and QDs-FLC composites [70].
Temperature dependence of (a) optical tilt, (b) switching time, and (c) spontaneous polarization (Ps) for neat FLC and of CDs doped FLC composites [71].
Frequency and concentration dependent ac conductivity for neat FLC and CDs doped FLC composites at 30Ȃ°C [71].

The measuring ac voltage-dependent dielectric constant (ε′) and dielectric loss factor (tanδ) at room temperature of (a) and (b) pure FLC (S1), (c) and (d) CDs dispersed FLC (i.e. bulk) nanocomposite (S2), and (e) and (f) CDs deposited on the substrate of the surface (S3). The FLC sample is KCFLC 7S. The thickness of S1, S2, and S3 sample cells was kept ~8 μm [72].

The black and white image shows the sample in polarising optical microscope (POM) contrast (magnification 40 ×); the specific and representative area tested by FCM is indicated by a red square. The (x, z) and (x, y) scans correspond to each other as indicated by the red lines. The blue square gives a higher-resolution image of the marked region [73].

List of Cd free QDs dispersed in different types of LCs.

row.entry: • Self-alignment of QDs with homeotropic or homogeneous states using different LC cell fabrication methods. • Superior electro-optical characteristics of a reduced Vth and the fast response times for both vertically aligned (VA) and electrically controlled birefringence (ECB) mode LC cells with controllable device switching

• Reduction in order parameter of FLC, and exponential decrease in spontaneous polarization (Ps), the cone angle (2θ) • ~52% and 92% enhancement in electro-optical (E-O) responses at 4 V for mixture 2 and mixture 3, respectively • Nonlinearity in dielectric properties • Increase in the photoluminescence with increasing temperature

• Enormous enhancement (~35%) in optical birefringence • Decrease in various physical parameters such as switching time, splay elastic constant (K11), and rotational viscosity (γ) • Enhanced dielectric anisotropy and better switching temporal response • Higher conductivity
Improvement in electro-optical properties of FLC
Sustained multiferroic behavior with the intrinsic ferroelectric nature
Size dependent tuning of PL intensity and blue shift in peak position of PL spectra of ZnO QDs-FLC composites
ZnS QDs (2.25 nm), ZnS:Mn (20 mol.%) QDs-2.45 nm and ZnS:Mn (40 mol.%) QDs- 2.6 nm

• Appearance of new dielectric relaxation mode in case of 40 mol% Mn doped ZnS QDs-FLC mixture • Remarkably larger value for apparent tilt angle for mixtures 2 and 3 and net enhancement in Ps value • Non-linear PL response
• Enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity and shift in spectral energy band of FLC doped with ZnS QDs
• Remarkable enhancement in switching time, spontaneous polarization, dielectric constant and dc conductivity • Suppression of Goldstone mode
• Modulation of dielectric properties of FLC

• Decrease in threshold voltage, dielectric anisotropy, switching response time and elastic constants (K11 and K33) • Increase in rotational viscosity (γ) • Increase in pre-tilt angle at low temperature
CLC (Nematic LC, MDA-03–3970 with left-handed chiral dopant S811)
• Achievement of AIPQD doped Cholesteric liquid crystal lasing cavity laser which can be very easily tuned and has long-term stability.

head.ce:title: Recent advances on cadmium free quantum dots-liquid crystal nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Liquid crystal (LC) nanoscience has been witnessing a paradigm shift towards the dispersion of Cadmium (Cd) based quantum dots (QDs) into LCs to enhance their electro-optical properties and produce highly efficient tunable electro-optical devices. However, the severe toxicity of Cd based QDs impedes their technological impact on the LC based tunable electro-optical devices and hence demands for the usage of Cd-free QDs. Here, we present a critical review on recent accomplishments and insights in the Cd-free QDs doped LC nanocomposites. The investigations on these nanocomposites have clearly demonstrated that the core/shell structure QDs (CIS/ZnS, InP/ZnS and Co-ZnO/ZnO), ZnO and ZnS based QDs, Carbon Dots, and perovskite-quantum dots possess the strong potentials to greatly modulate the properties of LCs and hence could be used as alternative eco-friendly dopants. Moreover, the focus has been made on summarizing how the size and shape dependent optical and electronic properties of Cd-free QDs influence the electrical, electro-optical and photoluminescent properties of different types LCs (i.e. nematic, chiral nematic and ferroelectric). The overview on inclusive existing status, challenges, and future scope of Cd-free QDs-LC nanocomposites has also been discussed. The present review indicates that LC nanocomposites made of Cd-free QDs hold a great promise for their futuristic applications in various robust, low cost, tunable and sustainable electro-optical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid Crystal (LC) is an intermediate state of matter between a crystalline solid and an isotropic liquid (Fig. 1 ) [1]. They possess many of the mechanical properties of isotropic liquid such as high fluidity, formation, and coalescence of droplets, etc. and at the same time, exhibit large anisotropy in their optical, mechanical, electrical, and magnetic properties as in the crystalline solids. The characteristic feature of LCs is the presence of long-range orientational order in the arrangement of constituent molecules, and sometimes one or two dimensional quasi long-range translational (or positional) order. LCs exhibits a great variety of phases, which differ one from another by their structure and physical properties [2]. For display applications, LC materials with rod-shaped molecules are the most commonly used even till today. The molecules of LCs are typically rod-shaped organic moieties ~ (having length 25 Å approx.) and their ordering is a function of temperature. The LCs materials which show different LC phases (nematic and smectic) on the variation in temperature are known as thermotropic LCs.

The nematic (N) phase, for example, is characterized by the long range orientational order of the constituent molecules i.e. all the molecules on the average oriented towards a common direction defined as director (n). The molecular orientation (i.e. director n) can be controlled with applied electric/magnetic/optical fields leading to the tunable electrical, electro-optical and optical properties of N phase. Most importantly, nematics are still the most commonly used LC phase in liquid crystal displays (LCDs), with many such devices using the twisted nematic geometry. The presence of chiral molecules in N phase causes a twist in the nematic structure and form a helical phase defined as a cholesteric LC or chiral nematic (N*) phase. Locally, the cholesteric LCs are very similar to the nematic LCs. They consist of quasi-nematic layers, whose individual directors are turned by a fixed angle on proceeding from one layer to the next. Although the nematic and cholesteric LCs are similar in their basic structures, the optical properties of the cholesterics are significantly different due to the strong twisting. In a certain spectral range, cholesteric LCs show a selective reflection of the circularly polarized light of the same handedness. The smectic phases, which are found at lower temperatures than the nematic, form well-defined layers that can slide over one another like soap. Thus smectics would have positional order in addition to orientational order. In smectic A phase (Sm A, simplest smectic phase), molecules (i.e. director n) are oriented along the layer normal (k), while in smectic C (Sm C) phase,director n is tilted with respect to k .There are also some tilted phases which have hexatic in-plane ordering, such as smectic I (Sm I) and smectic F (Sm F) as well as various crystalline smectics. Most importantly, the chiral tilted smectic phases (Sm C*, Sm F*, etc.) possess the spontaneous polarization (PS) and known as ferroelectric liquid crystals (FLCs). FLCs respond much more quickly to applied fields than nematics do and can be used to make fast and bistable (surface stabilized) electro-optic devices [3]. Various LC phases (especially N, N* and FLCs) have shown their potential applications in display (digital watches, calculators, LCD panels, TV, etc.) and non-display devices (lasers, sensors, filters, etc.) [4-9].

In today's cut throat competition among display technologies, nanotechnology is playing a pivotal role in the enhancement of device performance. Nanomaterials possess remarkable characteristics features such as unusual optical, magnetic, electrical properties, etc. Because of such unique properties, nanomaterials find potential applications in the field of electronics, photonics, spintronics, medical applications and other devices [10-12]. Integrating these properties in one material system is a fundamental challenge. To take up this challenge, in the recent past, dispersion of nanomaterials in LCs has emerged as new cost-effective route to obtain suitable nanocomposites that have strong potential to be used in producing displays, and other electro-optical devices with lower threshold voltage, faster switching speed, enhanced optical contrast, enlarged viewing angle, etc. Metallic nano particles [13-14] semiconducting nano particles such as quantum dots [15, 16], ferroelectric nano particles [17], carbon nano particles [18-21] and the other materials like polymer, dye molecules, etc. [22-23] are being dispersed in various LC systems in order to enhance the physical properties of LCs eventually useful in next generation displays and other photonic devices. The main thrust of research in this arena is to (a) to produce highly stable, uniform and defect-free liquid crystal nanocomposites with enhancement of physical properties of LCs, (b) understand the plausible interaction mechanism among the LCs and nanomaterials leading to impact the properties of LCs and (c) self-organisation of nanomaterials using the self-assembly properties of anisotropic LCs.

Few good reviews are available in literature, which have broadly covered various features of liquid crystal quantum dots composites [15, 16]. Study clearly shows that quantum dots (QDs) dispersion in LCs has gained extreme attention because QDs are highly energy efficient, possess highly tunable optical properties and required in a very small quantity. One can obtain emission of specific color from QDs by changing their sizes without changing the material. Dispersing QDs in LCs is an innovative technique to consumers without the high expense or complexity of creating new fabrication processes. From the last one-decade, various research groups around the globe are using this technology to greatly improve the properties of various photonic devices via dispersion of various QDs in LCs [24,25]. Among all types of QDs, Cd based QDs have especially gained much popularity due to their high quantum efficiency and narrow emission line width. However, the potential health and environmental impacts of Cd and other heavy metal based QDs have been raising serious concerns with toxicologists and regulators [26-28]. The adverse effect of the toxicity of QDs on almost all major organs of body has been reported. As per the directions of the European Union issued in 2003, known as the Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS) [29], maximum Cd content is limited to 100 ppm in any consumer electronic product. Moreover, there are variations in units of measurements for permissible dosage/exposure concentrations of Cd QDs reported in the literature (e.g., micrograms per milliliter, molarity, milligrams per kilogram body weight, QDs per cell). The reported values of dose–response relationships can be found in reference [26].

Therefore, there is an increasing demand for manufacturers to eliminate Cd along with other heavy metals from the consumer products. In order to develop and promote sustainable and responsible technologies, there is a need to develop Cd-free QDs (i.e. eco-friendly QDs) dispersed LCs nanocomposites-based electro-optical and other photonic devices. This review primarily focuses on the recent advances made in the use of Cd-free QDs as dopant in LC medium. The effects of Cd free QDs on LC alignment and the corresponding effect on switching response, dielectric properties, tuning of PL spectra etc. reported so far are prominent and show promise as an alternate for Cd based QDs for various electro-optical applications. The review begins with brief summary about QDs followed by the methodologies employed for the preparation of QD-LCs nanocomposites. Afterwards, review has been divided into four sections depending upon the types of Cd free QDs used as dopants in LC matrix i.e. (i) core/shell quantum dots dispersed liquid crystals, (ii) ZnO and ZnS based quantum dots dispersed liquid crystals, (iii) carbon dots dispersed liquid crystals and (iv) perovskite-quantum dots dispersed liquid crystals. We will discuss the effect of QDs dispersion on molecular alignment, memory effect, dielectric, optical and electro-optical properties of different types of LCs in each section respectively. These studies are certainly useful in order to produce tunable and eco-friendly high-performance photonics and other electro-optical devices. Such nanocomposites would be conducive test beds to understand the interaction among QDs and LCs leading to modified physical properties of LCs as well as of QDs. Although the science of liquid crystal nanocomposites is advancing with pace, direct application of these findings in electro-optical devices especially LCDs is limited to the laboratory only. It appears that some more time would be required to push the application of QDs-LC nanocomposites in LCDs from laboratory to industry level. Therefore, this review would provide an update to the researchers about the potentials of Cd-free QDs-LC nanocomposites and alert to expedite the use of these nanocomposites in LCDs and other photonic devices at industrial scale.

Optical, electronic, mechanical and various other physical and chemical properties of bulk material changes abruptly when one or more of their dimensions are reduced to the nanoscale. By reducing the size of materials to the nanoscale, quantum confinement effect becomes prominent by confining the movement of charge carriers (electrons and holes) inside the material.

Depending upon the number of degrees of freedom experienced by charge carriers (electrons and holes) inside the material, nanostructures are classified into (i) two-dimensional (quantum well), (ii) one-dimensional (quantum wire) and (iii) zero-dimensional (quantum dot). The movement of charge carriers is confined in one dimension, two dimensions and three dimensions in 2D, 1D, and 0D nanostructures respectively. As a result of spatial confinement of charge carriers, the density of states becomes highly dependent on the size of the nanomaterial and discretization of electron's energy takes place. Effect of reduction of size of material on the density of states of electron in bulk, 2D, 1D and 0D semiconductor structure is shown in Fig. 2 .

Quantum dots (QDs) are zero dimensional semiconducting nanostructures whose diameter typically ranges between 10 and 100 atoms. Due to their exceptionally small size of the order of a few to a few tens of nanometers, there is reduction in the number of atoms in a material, which eventually results into strong confinement of electrons or electron-hole pair called ‘excitons’. To understand the concept of quantum confinement in QDs, let us first discuss about excitons. In bulk semiconductors, very large number of atoms are brought together result in the formation of nearly continuous bands of energy states. The outermost two energy bands, known as the valence band and the conduction band govern the electrical properties of the material. The difference in energies of these two bands is characteristic of the material and is known as “band gap”. In bulk semiconductors, conduction band is completely empty at 0 K and valence band is filled and energy of band gap is such that a photon of minimum energy hν (equals to bad gap energy of material) can excite electrons from the valence band into the conduction band where they are free to move leaving behind a hole. This excited electron recombines with the hole after a finite time with the release of energy. When the size of material is reduced to few nanometers, the electron and hole pair created by excitation interacts with each other forming a bound state known as ‘exciton’. This interaction is similar to interaction of electron and proton in a hydrogen atom and hence the distance between bound state of electron-hole exciton is known as ‘exciton Bohr radius’. When the size or diameter of semiconducting nanocrystals becomes comparable or less than the exciton Bohr radius, quantum confinement effect dominates leading to formation of energy levels in QDs [31-34]. These energy levels can be tuned by varying the size of QDs.

To excite an electron from one of n energy states in valence band to one of the n energy states in conduction band, a photon must possesses following amount of energy:

e and mh are effective mass of electron and hole respectively, d is the radius of QD. Electron and hole interact with each other, creating a negative potential energy given by last term in the Eq. (1) [35,36] Therefore Eq. (1) correlates band gap energy of QDs with its dimension (d). We can see that if d increases, band gap energy of QDs, EQD decreases which mean larger size QDs emit longer wavelength and vice versa (Fig. 3 ). This enables us to obtain any specific wavelength emission from QD by varying its size and this property has opened up a new design freedom [37-38]. Another desirable feature of QDs is their high-purity emission colors. The emission of a QD sample is the convolution of fluorescence emission of each individual QD in a population. Therefore, the line-width is determined by inhomogeneous broadening of QD particle size distribution. In other words, the relative size and size distribution of QDs can be determined using emission wavelength in fluorescence spectroscopy.

QDs-LC nanocomposites are mostly prepared by dispersing a minute amount of QDs, already dispersed in a solvent (typically ethanol) to the LC. Sometimes, an appropriate chemical agent is often used to encase the QDs in order to increase the solubility and solution stability of the QDs. After dispersion, the mixture is ultrasonicated for ~1 to 2 h at a temperature above the isotropic temperature of the LC in order to obtain a uniform dispersion. The solvent used to disperse the QDs is then evaporated by placing the nanocomposite under vacuum for about 24 to 48 h. Afterwards, the nanocomposite is filled into surface treated ITO patterned sample cells via capillary action. It is worth mentioning that this is one of the methods, mostly used to prepare QDs-LC nanocomposites and sample cells filled with such nanocomposites in order to carry out optical, dielectric and electro-optical characterizations. However, precautions are taken to ensure that (i) the concentration of QDs is not so high as to cause their aggregation, (ii) the temperature of nanocomposite is kept relatively low to avoid damage to the capping materials on the QDs and chemical decomposition of the LC compound, and (iii) the solvent is completely evaporated from the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cd based QDs dispersed LC nanocomposites: brief overview
ce:section.ce:para: QDs-LC nanocomposites are of great interest from last few years and the development of such hybrid systems is still undergoing very actively. In these nanocomposites, LCs and QDs are mutually benefitted from each other. For instance, on one hand QDs affect the molecular alignment, memory effect, dielectric, optical and electro-optical properties of LCs. On the other hand, photoluminescence of QDs in LC media would also be interesting to study due to the plausible self-assembly of QDs mediated through anisotropic ordering of LC molecules. As discussed earlier, the extensive work has been done by various research groups around the globe on Cd-based QDs-LC nanocomposites. There are high quality review articles available in literature [15, 16], which provide an overview of recent work done in this area. However, our focus in this article will be limited to heavy metal free (especially Cd) spherical QDs dispersed calamatic liquid crystals (nematic, cholesteric and ferroelectric LCs) only. Previous studies [39-52] show that size of capped Cd based QDs (CdS, CdSe and CdTe), length of alkyl chain, capping agent and concentration of added capped Cd based QDs plays pronounced role in modifying the alignment and electro-optical properties of nematic liquid crystals (NLCs). It has been observed that capped Cd based QDs usually induce homeotropic alignment in NLCs and FLCs and reduction in threshold voltage (Vth) and splay elastic constant (k11). Cd based core/shell QDs lead to reduction in switching time and threshold voltage (Vth) in NLC. In case of FLCs, concentration dependent pronounced effects of Cd based core/shell QDs have been observed on dielectric and electro-optical properties. Also, enhancement in memory, value of spontaneous polarization, rotational viscosity and change in alignment was also observed. Functionalised CdS and CdSe QDs have shown photorefractive effect and enhancement in diffraction grating in NLCs. An interesting phenomenon of electrically reversible modulation and switching of photoluminesce (PL) emission was observed in CdSe/ZnS-QDs dispersed cholesteric liquid crystal. Besides tunable PL of liquid crystal composites with QDs, macroscopic alignment of strongly emitting semiconductor quantum rods in liquid crystal polymer (LCP) matrix leading to tunable polarized emission, potential for advancement of LCDs, has also been reported [53-55].

For various applications where the toxicity of Cd based QDs limits their use, the use of Cd-free core/shell QDs provides an alternative to Cd based QDs. These Cd Free core/shell QDs possess nearly similar luminescent properties of other QDs because the core/shell structure provides the distinctive properties like high brightness, strong optical absorbance over a wide excitation wavelength range, photostability and defined emission spectra etc. Dispersion of such core/shell QDs in LCs have shown promising results as replacement of Cd based QDs for variety of electro-optical applications. In the hope to develop new LC composites with superior electro-optical features without alignment layer, Lee et al. investigated Cd-free core/shell CuInS2-ZnS QDs (size ~2.2 nm, 1D chain-like clusters) dispersed in room temperature NLC (5CB) nanocomposites [56]. They successfully demonstrated that LC materials could be aligned into homogeneous (planar) and homeotropic (vertical) configurations by CuInS2 (CIS)-ZnS (CIS-ZnS) QDs without using any conventional alignment layers generally used for the production of LCDs. The capabilities of QDs to induce homogeneous/homeotropic alignment were tested with sample cells filled with positive and negative dielectric anisotropic LCs (Fig. 4 ). Two distinct methods (drop cast and capillary force) were employed to fill the LC nanocomposites into sample cells. It was found that capillary force method could induce homogeneous alignment in the positive dielectric NLC, whereas drop cast method induce vertical alignment with negative dielectric NLC. Authors predicted that in drop cast method, 1D QD clusters diffused into micro defects on the ITO (Indium tin oxide) surface resulting into the minimization of surface energy and decrease in van der Waal's interactions which eventually lead to vertical alignment of negative dielectric NLC. However, in capillary force method, most of 1D QDs clusters aligned in the filling direction and causing NLC molecules to align in the same direction and such filling effect propagated into the bulk and eventually produced the planar alignment. Moreover, enhanced electro-optical properties were also obtained on these LC nanocomposites. For instance, in vertical alignment (VA) mode, threshold voltage (Vth) and switching response time (τ) were reduced by 47% and 38.3%, respectively. However, in the electrically controlled birefringence (ECB) mode, Vth and τ were reduced by 37.1% and 36.6%, respectively. The Vth of these two devices was also found to be tunable with concentration of QDs. It was further pointed out that the significant reduction in Vth could be due to the significant decrement in the elastic constant of NLC. Authors were successful in proposing a very simple and cost-effective method of dispersing CIS-ZnS QDs into NLCs to control their director alignment with enhanced electro-optical properties useful for the flexible LCD devices devoid of highly toxic Cd [56].

Moreover, the switching response time of FLC in mixtures (Mix. 1, Mix. 2 and Mix. 3) at 4 V, especially in Mix. 2 and Mix. 3 were found to be ~52% and 92% faster as compared to neat FLC sample, respectively. The rotational viscosity was decreased in mixtures with increase in concentration of QDs. Such ultra-fast response of FLC was attributed to the combined effects of the coupling of PS and applied electric field (E) and the contribution of a reduced viscous torque. The impact of electronic properties of QDs on the switching response time of FLC was completely discarded due to the semiconducting nature of QDs.

The capacitance of FLC in mixtures were found to be larger than neat FLC which clearly indicates that QDs increases its capacitance to store more electronic charges and hence mixtures would certainly have strong potential to store the electronic charges. The variation in capacitance of FLC in mixtures was found to be linear at lower voltages but become non-linear at higher voltages. It was mentioned that nonlinearity in dielectric properties could be explained on the basis of synergistic effect of induced orientation, intermolecular interactions, formation of aggregates and creation of new dipoles.

Moreover, the temperature dependent photoluminescence (PL) was also measured for neat FLC and QDs-FLC mixtures (Fig. 7 ). In neat FLC sample, PL intensity was found to decrease with increase in temperature (Fig. 7(a)) and such thermal quenching of PL was adequately explained by using Seitz equation [59-61] and theoretical model of Dorenbos [62]. In Mix. 1, PL intensity increased with increase in temperature in SmC* phase but decreased in the vicinity of SmC*-SmA* phase transition and beyond (Fig. 7(b)). The similar trend was also seen in Mix. 2 but the increase was not steeper than Mix. 1. Such increment in PL was corroborated with negative thermal quenching phenomenon proposed by Shibata model [63]. However, in Mix. 3, thermal quenching of PL was again observed in SmC* phase but PL intensity was found to increase in the vicinity of SmC*-SmA* phase transition and in SmA* phase (Fig. 7(c)). Authors predicted that the enhancement in PL in the proximity of SmC*-SmA* phase transition and in SmA* phase could be due to the defect-mediated spontaneous emission enhancement between FLC molecules and QDs. Moreover, very weak temperature-dependent blue and red shifts in PL emission were also observed in Mix. 2 and Mix. 3, respectively and could be due to the change in surface morphology observed in optical textures.

After the detail study about the photoluminescence properties of these QDs-FLC mixtures, authors were able to claim that Mix. 2 is suitable for better electro-optical devices and Mix. 3 for solar cell applications. These mixtures could serve as an alternate to Cd based QDs technology in practical device applications.

To completely avoid the risk related to Cd exposure and the toxicity of Cd based QDs, InP QDs are one of the most promising candidates. But because of thermal instability of pure InP QDs, they are usually coated with a shell. When these core/shell QDs are doped in LC matrix, encapsulation of core with shell actually plays the important role of elevating the luminescent properties of QDs-LC mixtures. Focusing on these facts, Roy et al. [64] did the detailed investigation of highly luminescent InP/ZnS QDs (core/shell, core ~3–4 nm) dispersed-medium birefringent NLC (1823A) composites. Two composites namely composite 1 and composite 2 were prepared by adding 0.1 and 0.2 ml diluted solution of QDs to NLC sample, respectively. The temperature-dependent birefringence measurements clearly showed that significant enhancement in birefringence in both composites as compared to neat NLC sample (Fig. 8 ). Interestingly, at room temperature, about 35% enhancement in birefringence was obtained in composite 1 and which was higher than composite 2.

Authors pointed out that the free electrons from optically enriched core provide the configurational alignment to the QDs-NLC composite system enhancing the phase difference and eventually increasing overall the birefringence of the NLC. The threshold voltage of NLC was significantly lowered in composites and found to be concentration dependent. Similarly, the splay elastic constant (K11) (Fig. 9 ) and rotational viscosity (γ) were also found to decrease with increase in the concentration of QDs and temperature. Moreover, the dielectric anisotropy was found to be significantly increased in composites as compared to neat NLC sample. Besides increased dielectric anisotropy, the ac conductivity was also enhanced in composites as compared to neat NLC sample. The authors anticipated that the plausible interaction between QDs and NLC and special role played by core/shell structure of QDs would produce the observed results.

In another report on Cd free core/shell QDs dispersed LC, Doke et al. synthesized Co-ZnO/ZnO core/shell (CZZ) QDs (~4–5 nm) with varying concentration of Co (0, 2, 5, and 10%) in ZnO by using wet chemical route and then dispersed into FLC (KCFLC 7S) and reported the significant improvement in electro-optical properties [65]. It was observed that CZZ QDs (especially CZZ-5 QDs) significantly improves the alignment of FLC molecules as compared to the neat FLC sample. The two order parameters of FLC, namely, tilt angle (θ) and PS were significantly enhanced due to the dispersed QDs (Fig. 10 ). The voltage-dependent tilt angle behavior clearly shows that increase in tilt angle and decrease in saturation voltage in the composites as compared to neat FLC and attributed to the strengthen anchoring of FLC molecules. PS is found to be significantly increased with increase in concentration of Co in core of CZZ QDs, which is indicative of increased ordering of FLC molecules. The rotational viscosity and phase transition temperature of FLC were also modified by CZZ QDs due to the simultaneous impact of order parameters and intermolecular interactions. The dielectric constant and dielectric loss of FLC were also greatly impacted by CZZ QDs. The photoluminescence intensity of FLC was also affected by CZZ QDs and it was optimum for CZZ-5 QDs and attributed to the enhanced molecular alignment. Authors believed that these Cd-free core/shell QDs-LC composites would pave the way forward for the fabrication of FLC based emissive displays with improved brightness, better contrast, wide viewing angle and lower operating voltage with longer self-life as compared to other QD technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnO and ZnS based quantum dots dispersed liquid crystals
ce:section.ce:para:
As already discussed, the most challenging aspect of working with QDs-LC mixtures for device applications is synthesis of weakly toxic QDs and especially Cd-free QDs. A promising member of the Cd-free QDs is ZnO QDs. ZnO QDs are known for their non-toxic nature and size dependent photoluminescent properties. Joshi et al. took the advantage of these facts and doped the different sizes of ZnO QDs (in the strong, medium and weak quantum confinement region) in a FLC (KCFLC7S) material and studied their photoluminescent properties. Authors reported the realization of tunable photoluminescent (intensity and spectral peak) FLC devices by doping ZnO QDs of varying size (~2.4 nm – 9 nm) in host FLC medium [67]. The PL intensity of FLC was found to increase (~4 fold to 13 fold) linearly with decrease in size of QDs. Enhancement in PL intensity was explained based on the larger surface contribution of smaller QDs and QD-FLC interactions and ordering of FLC molecules caused by presence of ZnO QDs. In addition to enhancement in PL intensity, a significant shift in PL peak position in QD-FLC composites was observed in comparison to pure FLC medium (Fig. 13
). Shift in peak position was observed to be size dependent. This was attributed to the change in the refractive index of FLC medium due to the doping of ZnO QDs. Hence, these non-toxic ZnO QDs-FLC mixtures provide tunable PL and could be used in fabrication of tunable photoluminescent FLC devices.

In addition to ZnO QDs, the ZnS QDs are also lesser toxic as compared to traditional QDs. Singh et al. synthesized pure ZnS QDs (~2.25 nm) and Mn doped ZnS (20 and 40 mol% Mn) QDs (~2.45 and 2.6 nm) using simple co-precipitation method and dispersed into a commercially available FLC material (Felix 17/000) and probed their impact on the phase transition, dielectric, electro-optical and photoluminescence properties of investigated FLC material [68]. Besides neat FLC sample, three mixtures of FLC with QDs namely mixture 1 (FLC+0.25 wt% ZnS QDs), mixture 2 (FLC+0.25 wt% Mn (20 mol%) doped ZnS QDs) and mixture 3 (FLC+0.25 wt% Mn (40 mol%) doped ZnS QDs) were prepared. The stability of prepared mixtures was confirmed through differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and optical polarising microscope. No remarkable shift in the phase transition temperature of the various phases of FLC material present in QDs-FLC mixtures were detected in both heating and cooling scans of DSC. There was also no visible distortion in the alignment of FLC observed in optical textures of mixtures. However, in mixture 3, micrometer sized aggregates of QDs were observed due to the maximum Mn-Mn dipolar interaction. Interestingly, a new dielectric relaxation mode (NM), Goldstone mode (GM, phason fluctuations) and ionic contribution were observed in mixture 3 (Fig. 9) and attributed to the formation of splay structures in the FLC around the QDs resulting into the flexoelectric tilt fluctuations. Some of the characteristics of newly discovered mode i.e. NM (distribution parameter, nature of relaxation frequency and relaxation strength) were matched with conventional soft mode. However, the DC bias dependence of new mode was opposite to soft mode. The ac conductivity measurements showed that it was reduced to half in mixture 3 as compared to neat FLC sample confirming the trapping of ionic contaminants by the QDs.

The electro-optical property of FLC (optical tilt angle, spontaneous polarisation, and switching response time) present in mixtures was also significantly tuned by QDs. The apparent optical tilt angle of FLC in mixtures 2 and 3 were significantly increased at low DC bias (2 to 12 V) and attributed to large dipole moments of Mn doped ZnS QDs and which eventually increased the effective electric field experienced by the FLC molecules. However, in mixture 1, no increment in optical tilt was monitored due to the higher conductivity in this mixture. The significant increment in spontaneous polarization of FLC was also obtained in mixtures and attributed to (i) the dipolar correlation between dipoles of FLC and QDs and (ii) flexoelectric polarization. The switching response time was lowered in all the mixtures and lowest in mixture 2. The PL intensity of FLC material (~390 nm) was found to decrease in mixtures 1, 2, and 3 (i.e. concentration-dependent PL quenching) but at the same time, there was no PL spectral shift observed. However, the PL intensity was increased in mixture 3 as compared to mixtures 1 and 2. Such non-linear behavior of PL intensity was attributed to the formation of Mn2+ luminescent centers in mixture 3. The changes in the physical properties of FLC mainly due to Mn doped ZnS QDs are indicative of the interaction among the FLC molecules and QDs. To explore such interactions, authors further carried out the FTIR of neat FLC and its mixtures with both types of QDs (Fig. 15 ). Results showed that QDs not only froze some intrinsic vibration bands but lead to appearance of few extrinsic vibration bands in range 2000–3000 cm−1 which eventually supported the strong interaction between ZnS QDs and FLC material. Authors pointed out that these Cd-free QDs-FLC mixtures would pave the new path to achieve faster switching response of the FLC, tunability of relaxation processes and quenching of PL by ditching the toxicity related to use of Cd based QDs.

In another report on utilizing non-toxic ZnS QDs, Kumar et al. [69] synthesized water-soluble MPA (3-mercaptopropeonic acid) capped ZnS QDs free of toxic elements in an environmentally friendly way without the use of organometallic precursors which possesses health and environmental hazards. Authors proudly reported the significant enhancement in the PL intensity and spectral shift in PL peak position of FLC (KCFLC 7S) by dispersing newly synthesized MPA capped ZnS QDs (~2–7 nm) using co-precipitation method (Fig. 16 ). Three composites of FLC-QDs were prepared by dispersion of 1, 3, and 5 µl ZnS QDs into ~ 3 mg of FLC material. An increase in PL intensity was observed in QD-FLC mixture with increasing concentration of QDs [Fig. 16(a)]. Moreover, the spectral shift in PL peak (low and higher wavelengths) towards longer wavelength side was observed [Fig. 16(b)]. It was mentioned that both ZnS QDs and FLC have some common wavelength span in their emission bands which when combined together eventually leads to the enhanced PL intensity of ZnS QDs/FLC composites.

The observed spectral shift in PL peak was attributed to the change in refractive index of the FLC molecules due to different concentrations of ZnS QDs in FLC matrix. Authors strongly believed that these Cd free ZnS QDs-FLC mixtures would certainly be useful in developing smart optoelectronic devices with tunable emission color.

In a pursuit of exploring further the interaction between ZnS QDs and FLC (SCE4) and the effect on optical properties of QD-FLC mixtures, Tripti et al. [70] studied the PL spectra of ZnS QDs dispersed-FLC composites (Mix.1 and Mix. 2). They monitored significant enhancement in PL intensity which could be attributed to the increased molecular ordering of FLC sample. The changes in FLC molecular alignment present in the QD-FLC composites was monitored through optical polarising micrographs and PL spectra. The full width at half maxima (FWHM) values of PL peaks in the composites show the presence of QDs in the composites which eventually favor the near band emissions. The spectral position of PL peaks (1 and 2) showed the red shift by 1 and 2 nm in neat FLC and Mix. 1, respectively. However, 3rd PL peak of Mix. 2 was red shifted by ~8 nm (Fig. 17 ). Authors believed that investigated QDs-FLC composites would play major role in the development of display technology.

In continuation to make contribution towards a better understanding of the underlying mechanism of interactions between CDs and LC, Urbanski et al. synthesized chemically and thermally robust carbon dots (core size~2.5 ± 0.5 nm) using thermal decomposition method and dispersed into phenylpyrimidine-based NLC, LC1 (Felix-2900–03) and prepared four composites with 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 wt% concentration of CDs [73]. The polarising optical microscopy, electro-optical (dynamic tests and numerical simulations) and fluorescence confocal microscopy were extensively employed on these composites to exploit the impact of Ds CDs into the physical properties of LC1. Authors observed that in all composites, clearing temperature (TN-I) was decreased but biphasic region (i.e. Isotropic + Nematic) was significantly increased with increase in the concentration of CDs. The threshold voltage, dielectric anisotropy, switching response time and elastic constants (K11 and K33) were found to decrease and rotational viscosity to increase in these composites. It was also monitored that at concentration ≥2.5 wt%, D CDs present at the interface of polyimide layer and LC molecules in planar sample cells, induces increasing initial pretilt angle upto 8ο at lower temperatures and their impact on boundary conditions decreases with increase in temperature. This was further confirmed through detailed fluorescence confocal microscopy, wherein the fluorescence of used CDs actually allowed the direct visualization of their distribution in bulk of the composites inside the planar cells and even pattern-like, segregation of dots at the interfaces of polyimide and LC1 molecules (Fig. 22 ). Authors proposed that these composites would be certainly useful for lower threshold emissive electro-optical devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cd free perovskite quantum dots dispersed liquid crystals
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, significant advancements have been made about the application of perovskite QDs and heavy metal free perovskite QDs have emerged as superstar materials for various optoelectronic device applications because they possess high luminescence quantum yield, sharp emission peak, and emission color tunability. Because of these remarkable properties, these materials are also exploited as an amplifying media in laser systems. Therefore, to utilize this property of all-inorganic lead-free perovskite QDs to adept as optical gain medium for lasing action, Chen et al. synthesized all-inorganic lead-free cesium tin halide perovskite QDs (AIPQDs) using low-cost solvothermal method and fabricated AIPQDs doped cholesteric liquid crystal (AIPQDs-CLC) lasers, wherein CLC works as optical resonator [74]. Authors proudly claimed that these lasers show the remarkable lasing characteristics such as low threshold ~150 nJ/pulse and narrow line width ~0.20 nm, far better than the conventional dye doped CLC lasers. It was attributed to the amalgamation of (i) quenching of photoluminescence (PL) due to the quantum confinement of AIPQDs and (ii) PL amplification due to the band-edge effect of the CLC distributed feedback resonator. Moreover, widely wavelength tunable lasing by varying the chiral dopant concentration (Fig. 23 ) and longer stability with respect to room temperature storage and high humidity are additional features of AIPQDs-CLC lasers, unlike exemplary light-emitting perovskite devices. Based on striking merits of AIPQDs-CLC lasers and added advantage of toxic Pb-free fabrication mixtures, authors anticipated their tremendous applications in various optoelectronic and photonic devices such as lighting, displays and lasers.

The doping of Cd-free QDs into different types of LCs (nematic, cholesteric and ferroelectric) has greatly impacted their optical, electro-optical and dielectric properties, certainly useful for the tunable and high performance displays and other photonic devices based on LCs. For instance, CIS-ZnS QDs (core/shell structure) could (i) self-align NLCs into homogenous and homeotropic configurations with superior electro-optical features as compared to neat NLCs and (ii) reduction in order parameters (tilt angle and spontaneous polarization), concentration-dependent enhancement in electro-optical response, nonlinear dielectric behaviour and enhanced photoluminescence of FLCs. InP/ZnS QDs (core/shell structure), another important Cd-free QDs, could significantly enhance the optical birefringence, dielectric anisotropy and reduce switching response, rotational viscosity, and elastic constants of a room temperature NLC. CZZ QDs (Co-ZnO/ZnO, core/shell) could also significantly improve electro-optical features and demonstrate the sustained multiferroic behaviour with intrinsic ferroelectric nature of FLC. ZnO QDs were reported to tune the PL intensity and spectral peak of FLCs. The other Cd-free QDs (ZnS, Mn doped ZnS and MPA capped ZnS) were able to enhance the PL (intensity and spectral peak shift), nonlinear PL, enhanced tilt angle and induce new relaxation mode in FLCs. CDs which are Cd-free and very less toxic could also enhance the physical properties of NLC. The enhancement in switching response time, spontaneous polarization, dielectric constant, dc conductivity, suppression of Goldstone mode and p-UHM of FLC were induced by the doped CDs. Moreover, the heavy metal free all-inorganic perovskite QDs (CsSnI3, all-inorganic perovskite, AIP) i.e. AIPQDs were utilized to fabricate AIPQDs-doped lasers with cholesteric liquid crystal lasing cavities. Such lasers were found to be largely tunable lasing wavelength and long term stability at room temperature.

The investigations done on Cd-free QDs doped LC composites clearly demonstrate that these QDs are equally capable to amplify the physical properties of LCs like Cd based QDs, but at the same time keeping the level of toxicity as low as possible. Therefore, Cd-free QDs could replace Cd based QDs and serve as the best dopants to the LCs to produce eco-friendly LC nanocomposites to realize high performance tunable LCDs and other photonic devices. However, most striking questions still to be answered are: (i) why the same QD which could largely tune the properties of one LC material could not tune the properties of other LC material ?, (ii) could we optimize the conditions such as shape, size, capping, concentration and type of material used for Cd-free QDs to produce the optimum enhancement in the properties of LCs ?, and (iii) what is the exact physical mechanism involved in the interaction among Cd-free QDs and LC molecules leading to the affected properties of LCs ? It would be worth trying to functionalize Cd-free QDs with mesogens as it might be helpful in achieving more uniform and stable LC nanocomposites. Although, the results on Cd-free QDs doped LC nanocomposites are very profound, the devices based on them are appeared to be far away from the commercialization. Therefore, a lot more research on these LC nanocomposites is required to be done to push them from laboratory to the real world applications.
Fig. 14 and Table 1 .
